attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-day history of an itchy rash on her trunk and thighs, sparing her face. She reports attending a spa party three days ago and drank heavily on that night. She states that she had protected sexual intercourse with a man she met at the party. Her recent sexual transmitted infection screen was done six months ago at her request after unprotected sexual intercourse and it was negative. She has mild eczema and uses hydrocortisone cream once per month and the combined oral contraceptive pill.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple erythematous papules and pustules distributed across her trunk and thighs. The surrounding skin appears normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of Pseudomonas folliculitis (spa pool or hot tub folliculitis), caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa. The characteristic distribution of the rash in areas covered by swimwear, onset within 48 hours of hot tub exposure, and appearance of follicular-based pustules are diagnostic. There can be tender lymphadenopathy and burning sensation with this rash, as well as mild fatigue, mild fever or diarrhoea.

Pseudomonas folliculitis typically occurs after exposure to contaminated water, particularly in hot tubs where the warm temperature and humid environment promote bacterial growth. The rash is usually self-limiting and resolves within 7-10 days without specific treatment.

Syphilis typically presents with a painless chancre in primary infection, or a generalised maculopapular rash in secondary infection. The timing and appearance of this rash, along with the clear relationship to hot tub exposure, recent negative STI screen, make syphilis unlikely.

Tinea corporis typically presents with annular, scaly plaques with central clearing and advancing edges. It would not present with pustules and would not have such a clear temporal relationship with hot tub use.

Contact dermatitis could occur from chemicals in pool water especially in atopic patients, but typically presents with erythema, scaling, and intense pruritus rather than discrete pustules. The distribution would likely be more diffuse rather than concentrated in swimsuit-covered areas.

Impetigo is a bacterial skin infection that typically presents with honey-coloured crusts and would not have such a clear relationship with water exposure. It is also more common on exposed areas like the face and extremities.
learningPoints: Pseudomonas folliculitis typically presents 1-2 days after exposure to contaminated water, particularly hot tubs or swimming pools.
The characteristic rash consists of pruritic, erythematous papules and pustules predominantly in areas covered by swimwear.
Most cases are self-limiting and resolve within 7-10 days without specific treatment.
references: Jacob JS, Tschen J. Hot Tub-Associated Pseudomonas Folliculitis: A Case Report and Review of Host Risk Factors. Cureus. 2020.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7584313/
Oakley A, Al-Ani Z, Jarrett P, Mitchell G. Spa pool folliculitis.  DermNet NZ. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/spa-pool-folliculitis
